title: methodology
text:
Reconstructing a Hidden Archive
How many Chinese immigrants served in the American Civil War, and what can we learn about their lives?
Attempting to answer that question required building a research database that brings together scattered historical records from multiple archives and digital collections. Because Chinese soldiers were rarely documented in a consistent way, their stories often appear only in fragments across different sources. The database was created first to help myself organize the material, and now as a way to make them easier to analyze and share.
Identifying the Soldiers
The first step in building the database was identifying potential Chinese soldiers in Civil War records. Fortunately, the American Battlefield Trust and the National Park Service in 2015 released a pamphlet about Asian participation in the war. In this pamphlet were lists of soldiers by name, and it was here that I began my research. After narrowing down my target list to researching Chinese soldiers serving in both the Union and the Navy, I began my work.
Each soldier was cross-checked across multiple sources to confirm their identity wherever possible–Fold3, National Archives Catalog, Ancestry, FamilySearch, and FindAGrave. Nineteenth-century records were inconsistent, and identifying individuals usually required comparing spelling variations and alternate names. If I came across a record that sounded similar to the name I was looking to confirm, I made a note of it as a possibility. A single soldier might appear under various spellings depending on the official recording his information. Once I located the soldier, I used the information I found to begin searching for any mention of them in newspapers and printed media. One soldier could take hours.

title: methodology
text:
Reconstructing a Hidden Archive
How many Chinese immigrants served in the American Civil War, and what can we learn about their lives?
Attempting to answer that question required building a research database that brings together scattered historical records from multiple archives and digital collections. Because Chinese soldiers were rarely documented in a consistent way, their stories often appear only in fragments across different sources. The database was created first to help myself organize the material, and now as a way to make them easier to analyze and share.
Identifying the Soldiers
The first step in building the database was identifying potential Chinese soldiers in Civil War records. Fortunately, the American Battlefield Trust and the National Park Service in 2015 released a pamphlet about Asian participation in the war. In this pamphlet were lists of soldiers by name, and it was here that I began my research. After narrowing down my target list to researching Chinese soldiers serving in both the Union and the Navy, I began my work.
Each soldier was cross-checked across multiple sources to confirm their identity wherever possible–Fold3, National Archives Catalog, Ancestry, FamilySearch, and FindAGrave. Nineteenth-century records were inconsistent, and identifying individuals usually required comparing spelling variations and alternate names. If I came across a record that sounded similar to the name I was looking to confirm, I made a note of it as a possibility. A single soldier might appear under various spellings depending on the official recording his information. Once I located the soldier, I used the information I found to begin searching for any mention of them in newspapers and printed media. One soldier could take hours.

title: Race and Recognition
text:
Much like today’s military system, the Civil War military depended on paperwork. Every soldier generated records: enlistment forms, muster rolls, payrolls, hospital registers, prisoner lists, pension files, and more. These documents were meant to track soldiers, pay them, and record for their service. But beyond that, these records also helped define race and belonging.
Historians increasingly argue that the military bureaucracy of the nineteenth century contributed to the production of racial categories. While these systems were at first designed to organize soldiers, it also required officials to classify individuals according to race, nationality, and legal status. In doing so, the army helped turn fluid identities into fixed administrative labels. Many recruits, however, did not fit neatly into the fixed classifications the army expected. Immigrants, Indigenous soldiers, and other minority groups complicated these categories. Officials had to decide how to record names, describe physical features, and determine where soldiers fit within the army’s racial hierarchy.
But these decisions were not without consequences. Racial classification shaped much of the soldier’s experience– where they were assigned, how they were paid, promotion eligibility, and how their service would be remembered. Paperwork and administrative procedure therefore became a strong tool for organizing recognition.


Archival Limitations and Struggles
Names were frequently misspelled or anglicized. Unfamiliar with foreign spellings, clerks often wrote them down phonetically or used simplified alternatives. It made their jobs easier, but as a result, one individual could appear under multiple spellings across different documents. For example, “John Achoo” could also be “John Afoo”. Other records attempted to place soldiers into broad racial categories that did not accurately reflect their identities. Historian Angela He argues that military bureaucracies often functioned as a “racial laboratory,” forcing individuals into predetermined categories that shaped how race itself was understood.
These records also determined how soldiers could later claim recognition. After the war, veterans relied on military paperwork to prove their service when applying for pensions or benefits. How accessible these records were determined whether former soldiers received support from the federal government. For marginalized soldiers, this process could be equated to a nightmare. Missing files, inconsistent names, and racial prejudice made it much harder to establish eligibility.
As Historians 
Today, historians rely on these same documents to reconstruct the lives of Civil War soldiers, but the records themselves reflect the biases and limitations of the institutions that created them. Knowing this requires scholars to read the archive in a specific way.
Military paperwork, for example, could not be read as neutral evidence, but rather part of the process through which race, citizenship, and belonging were defined during the Civil War. This period saw the formation of the nation’s first draft, and with that, the first rudimentary system for classifying individuals for military service. For Chinese soldiers, they were placed into the existing categories of “white,” “black,” or “mulatto,” purely at the discretion of the enlisting officer or enumerator.  Beyond preserving history, the archive also reveals how the state attempted to organize and interpret human difference. Ruthanne Lum McCunn notes that racial classifications varied geographically– one state might have classified Chinese men as “mulatto” while another might have identified them as “white.” These inconsistencies in how Chinese immigrants were categorized reveals the shifting ground they found themselves on prior to 1870.
The Civil War forced the United States to confront questions about who belonged in the nation and how that belonging could be proven. Often, military records were one of the places where those questions could be answered. By looking at how both the army and the navy classified and documented soldiers, historians can see how ideas about race and citizenship were not only debated on battlefields, but were recorded in the paperwork of the state.

title: American Perceptions of Immigrants
text:
By the time the Civil War began in 1861, the United States was already a nation shaped by immigration. Millions of newcomers had arrived in the decades before the war. Smaller communities from China, Latin America, and other parts of the world were also beginning to establish themselves in American cities and ports.
Despite the arrivals, many Americans viewed immigrants with suspicion, and questions about language, culture, and loyalty sparked debates about belonging in the nation. Political movements such as the Know-Nothing Party in the 1850s openly argued that immigrants threatened American political institutions and social stability. In newspapers and propaganda, immigrants were often portrayed in newspapers and political rhetoric as outsiders who could not fully become American.
Of course, all immigrants experienced this hostility in different ways. European immigrants, while still facing discrimination, could eventually claim inclusion within the category of “white.” For immigrants outside of this category, acceptance was far more difficult.
Chinese migrants in particular faced intense racial prejudice. Many Americans described them as permanent foreigners incapable of assimilating into American culture. Newspapers frequently depicted Chinese immigrants using racialized stereotypes that emphasized the difference in language, appearance, and culture.  PHOTO EXAMPLES. These attitudes were already widespread decades before the passage of the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882, the first major U.S. law to restrict immigration based on nationality.


Immigrants and the Civil War
Despite these tensions, immigrants played a major role in the Civil War. Thousands of foreign-born men served in the Union army, and smaller numbers joined Confederate forces. There were even entire regiments organized around immigrant communities, including Irish, German, and other ethnic units. The Civil War unfolded at a moment when Americans were already arguing about the meaning of citizenship, and immigrant soldiers stepped directly into this debate. By fighting for the United States, they asserted their right to belong even as the society around them remained deeply divided.

title: About the Project
text:
About This Site
Although Chinese migrants have been present in the United States since the early 19th-century , their contributions to national conflicts—especially the Civil War—are rarely acknowledged in mainstream historical narratives. This omission not only perpetuates the stereotype of Chinese individuals as passive or apolitical immigrants but also distorts the historical record of U.S. military history and racial policy development. This research addresses a significant gap by documenting and analyzing Chinese participation in the Civil War.
While scholarship on African American, Native American, and even European immigrant soldiers has grown, Asian American military history remains underexplored—particularly in the pre-20th-century context. By situating Chinese service within the political, social, and legal frameworks of the Civil War era, this study expands our understanding of how military service intersected with racial hierarchies and citizenship policies. Moreover, this project contributes to Asian American studies by re-centering Chinese agency in foundational moments of U.S. history. It challenges the linear narrative that frames Asian Americans as only gaining political visibility in the 20th century and reinterprets the Civil War as a site of Asian American political engagement and contestation.


About the Researcher
The website is designed to make this research accessible to a wider audience. Through soldier profiles, historical essays, maps, and archival documents, the project invites visitors to explore the experiences of Chinese Civil War soldiers and the broader questions that their stories raise. Advised by Dr. Paul Quigley, this project was created by Madison Smith, a graduate student in the History M.A. program at Virginia Tech. Her research focuses on nineteenth-century American legal history, immigration, race, and citizenship, with particular attention to the ways laws and institutions shaped the experiences of Asian immigrants in the United States.


Note From the Researcher
This project began as part of my Master’s portfolio during my time at Virginia Tech where it was first conceptualized as a paper. As I continued the research process, I realized that, while the National Park Service has a fantastic project dedicated to this field of study, there is little more than documentation of names. In attempting to better understand the lives of these soldiers, I began my own database to track each man and compile a more useful and condensed history that included scouring the archives for every bit of information I could find. It soon became clear that a paper was not the most sufficient output, but a website. This is the product, and is, by no means, complete.


An Ongoing Process
Research on Chinese Civil War soldiers is ongoing. New records continue to surface as historians explore archives and digitized collections. Recent scholarship has especially highlighted the contributions made by marginalized groups during the Civil War era, and this project represents one step in a broader effort to recover the lives of individuals whose stories have long been overlooked in traditional narratives of Civil War.

title: John Hang
text:
John (Ah) Hang’s (Tong Kee Hang) life offers one of the most detailed and revealing stories of a Chinese immigrant who served during the American Civil War. Hang’s actual birth year and age was questioned during his entire life, but he alleged that he had been born in Canton, China, on July 17, 1841. He eventually made his way to the United States in 1857 at about 16 years of age. Prior to his service in the U.S. Hang claimed to work as a Steward on the vessel Shakespeare.
Hang enlisted on July 24, 1863, serving aboard the USS Albatross and USS Penguin. He worked as both a landsman and cabin steward, positions that placed him within the daily labor systems that sustained naval operations. In an interview given years later, Hang explains that his name was written down as “John Ah Hang” when he enlisted, but that this was the Chinese way of pronouncing it.
Hang claims that, at the time, he did not know enough about English to remember the names of the officials on the boat, but he remembered exactly what duties he completed. While on the Albatross, Hang assisted in blockading duty around Mobile Bay for about 8-9 months before transferring to the Penguin. There, he acted as Cabin Steward. He recalled the time that a couple of men on the ship became very sick and were taken to the hospital, and although he did not know the name of the sickness, he was sure it was not yellow fever. While landsmen performed general ship duties, cabin stewards often served officers directly. Hang remained in service until his final discharge on September 30, 1864 at Boston, Massachusetts, surviving the war and transitioning into civilian life.
After the war, Hang built a life in New York City. He worked as a tobacco merchant, operating businesses on Pearl Street and Mott Street, and later resided at the Soldiers’ and Sailors’ Home as he aged. His first application for a pension was successful, although there was an investigation held in 1908 after an author writing about the Chinese residents in New York requested an inquiry. This resulted in an investigator transcribing his interview with Hang in which he recounts his life story up until that point. His pension was resumed after this. His successful pension application demonstrates that he was able to secure federal recognition for his wartime service—something many immigrant veterans struggled to achieve.
Yet Hang’s life also reveals the limits of that recognition. Despite his military service and long residence in the United States, he attempted to naturalize under the name William Hang and succeeded, however it was taken away for him shortly after. His experience reflects the broader exclusion of Chinese immigrants from American citizenship in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.
Hang’s personal life further illustrates his integration into American society. He married twice—first to Jennie Busch in Savannah, Georgia, in 1868, and later to Maggie Duffy in 1878. Both marriages connect him to diverse immigrant communities, highlighting the interconnected social worlds of port cities like New York and Savannah. Unfortunately both of his marriages ended with him widowed, and he never remarried nor had any children. No pictures of his likeness exist.

title: Chinese Soldiers in the American Civil War
Robert Spicer
text:
Robert Spicer: A Sailor from Shanghai https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/116775306/robert-spicer/photo
Robert Spicer’s journey to the American Civil War began far from the battlefields of the United States. Born in Shanghai, China, in 1842, Spicer eventually found himself serving in the Union Navy during the Civil War. He enlisted on July 21, 1862 in New London, Connecticut as a landsman aboard the USS Sabine, a Union vessel, and was discharged on July 19th, 1863. His experience reflects a broader theme explored in this project: how immigrants and minority soldiers used military service to claim recognition in a nation that often viewed them as outsiders. U.S.S. Sabine, unknown date Unlike many participants who served in army regiments, Spicer fought at sea.
While historians often focus on immigrant regiments in the Union army, men like Spicer entered the conflict through the Union Navy. For sailors living in port towns, naval service was like a natural extension of their existing work. However, landsmen were among the lowest-ranking sailors in the navy, often young men with little previous maritime experience who were learning the demands of naval service during wartime.

Census records and print media reveal elements of Spicer’s life before and after the war. Newspaper accounts reported that he was brought to the United States in 1856 as a child by the wealthy sea-captain, Elihu Spicer, who purchased the boy from his parents in China for eight dollars. While the exact circumstances of this transaction remain difficult to verify, such arrangements were not uncommon in port communities like Mystic and New London, where sailors, merchants, and laborers regularly moved between Asia and the United States. Unfortunately, Spicer’s original Chinese name has been lost, but records show that he did adopt Capt. Spicer’s surname, and took “Robert” for his first. It was likely this coastal connection, as well as the fact that Captain Spicer himself became the captain of a Union steamship, that made enlistment in the Navy more accessible for him.
The 1860 census listed him as a seventeen-year-old laborer, showing that he began working before the outbreak of the conflict. After his service, Spicer remained in Connecticut and continued working along the coast. On July 16, 1875, he married Alice Somers at St. Mark’s Church. An 1880 census lists his occupation as a fisherman, reflecting a life tied closely to maritime labor. Spicer also sought payment and recognition for his service, as in 1889, he applied for and received a federal pension. These pensions were benefits for aging veterans that provided financial support.
According to a certificate later filed by his wife, he suffered for years from chronic illness, including fever, “ague”, and persistent diarrhea that she believed was brought on by his service. The couple lived in the coastal community of Noank, Connecticut for the remainder of their lives.
Alice Somers was born in 1850 in Ireland, and immigrated to the United States with her family when she was four years old. The couple did not have any children. Robert Spicer died on March 26, 1900, and newspapers remembered him as a well-known figure– nicknamed “Bob”– in the community and a respected veteran. His funeral was attended by the Grand Army of the Republic, the nation’s largest Union veteran’s association, of which Spicer was also a member. He is buried in Noank Cemetery. His wife, Alice Somers Spicer, would join him in his rest on February 24th, 1915.
Historical Interpretation
Newspaper accounts describing Spicer reveal how racial identity shaped American public perceptions of immigrant veterans. One obituary referred to him as a “naturalized Chinaman”, while another incorrectly described him as being of Japanese descent. These two descriptions alone demonstrate the uncertainty that surrounded immigrants in nineteenth-century America. Even when discussing individuals like Spicer who lived in the United States for decades, journalists still frequently emphasized their racial identity. However, the same articles also remembered Spicer as a respected member of the community, and all fondly noted his nickname–”Bob.” He was described as being well-liked in fishing circles and honored by the Grand Army of the Republic. This recognition suggests that military service was still able to provide immigrants with some degree of legitimacy, even when the society around them viewed them as perpetual outsiders. Through the limited documents we have, Spicer’s story can still be constructed to a fascinating degree. His life reflects the multi-facted realities faced by many immigrant veterans, and illustrates how global migration, wartime service, and local community intersected in the decades following the Civil War.
Newspaper references

“ROBERT SPICER’S FUNERAL. The funeral of the late Robert Spicer, known to every one as”Bob.” was emnized at the Baptist church yesterday afternoon. The arrangements were charge of the Grand Army, of which ceased was a member. A large number friends attended who accepted this the opportunity to attest to the high esteem which they yield this departed citizen. C. Martin preached the funeral sermon and spoke most feelingly, paying just ute to the memory of one who had always lived an upright life during his many residence in this place. The interment took place in Noank cemetery. “ “Robert Spicer’s Funeral” The Day, March 29th, 1900, pg. 3. https://www.newspapers.com/newspage/968766578/
“Robert Spicer Passed Away While Putting on His Clothing—News Matters.” Robert Spicer, a well known figure of place, dropped dead at his home on street, early this morning. He had no former signs of illness and had made rangements for a fishing trip today one of the local fishermen. He was for at the appointed hour but was partly dressed, lying on a sofa but was extinet. Mr. Spicer, more familiarly known “Bob,” was of Japanese descent and brought here from that country when small, by the late Capt. Elibu Spicer Mystic, who was then on a foreign It was so long ago that Bob has forgotten his mother tongue and spoke English language almost perfectly. original name was never known and correct age has always been a matter doubt, but when this country was he took the Spicer name with the name of Robert. He has always followed the water in ferent capacities but chiefly as steward was always well liked by his employers because of his faithfulness. He enlisted during the civil war and having served country honorably he received a small sion, which was his main support in latter years of his life “Bob” was a friend to every one and return every one was a friend to him. was quiet in his ways and liked by one and will be missed in fishing circles. He was a member in good standing of Grand Army post at Mystic, by which will probably be buried. He leaves a mourn his loss” “Robert Spicer Passed Away While Putting on His Clothing” The Day, March 26th, 1900, pg. 3. https://www.newspapers.com/newspage/968766399/
“NATURALIZED CHINAMAN WHO SERVED IN CIVIL WAR. Mystic, March Spicer was found dead in bed at his home in Noank early this morning. Heart failure is believed to have been the cause of death. Spicer, was a Chinaman and was brought to this country in 1856 by Captain Spicer, a millionaire shipowner and sea captain of this place, who purchased the boy from his parents in China. The amount involved in the transaction was $8. When the Civil war broke out the young man enlisted in the United States navy and served continuously until peace was restored. For many years Mr. Snider had enjoyed the rights of citizenship. He is survived by a widow.” “Naturalized Chinaman Who Served in Civil War” The Journal, March 26th, 1900, pg. 1. https://www.newspapers.com/newspage/968766399/

title: Tracing Civil War Journeys
text:
This interactive map traces documented and approximate journey stops for soldiers and sailors in the dataset. It is designed as a public-facing research tool: readers can explore movement across enlistment points, transfers, receiving ships, discharge locations, and other recorded stops.



This first version of the map emphasizes documented locations and carefully standardized place names rather than speculative routes. Lines connect each person’s stops in order using stop_order.



Lines trace each recorded journey in sequence.
Point colors distinguish exact, approximate, and uncertain locations.
Line colors distinguish Army and Navy records.
Clicking a point opens source-based details for that stop.

title: Tracing Civil War Journeys
section: Geocode places once and cache them
text:
Geocode places once and cache them
This chunk geocodes only places that do not already have latitude and longitude. It saves a reusable lookup table so you do not have to geocode everything every time you render.

title: Tracing Civil War Journeys
text:
Explore the map
Use the interactive controls to zoom, pan, and compare Army and Navy journeys. You can turn stops and journey lines on or off in the layer panel.

title: Sources
text:
Reconstructing the lives of Chinese soldiers who served during the American Civil War required careful and intentional work across many different historical records. Unlike famous, well-documented generals and leaders, many of these men appear only briefly in the historical archive, and oftentimes with only their name. As clerks were unfamiliar with Chinese names, they could also be in several different records under different names. Their stories exist in scattered documents created by the military, the government, newspapers, and later researchers and archivists. This project attempts to piece those fragments together.
Using military records, personal memoirs, and newspaper archives, this study attempts to reconstruct the experiences of Chinese soldiers and analyze their role in shaping military and racial policies. Sources such as military and government records (enlistment records, military reports, war correspondence and pension records) provide insight on identification and enrollment numbers and can be found in the National Archives and the U.S. War Department Reports as well as Fold3– a database made for searching for soldier records. Further, 19th-century newspapers, propaganda and recruitment materials, and documents describing political debates over the inclusion of Asian soldiers will provide a useful insight to track contemporary attitudes toward Asian military service.
Many soldiers in this project survive only in a single document like a muster roll, but leave no trace in later records. When working with marginalized communities, this archival silence is not unusual. Historical records often preserve the lives of some people more clearly than others. Language barriers, racial prejudice, poverty, and mobility made it more difficult, especially in this era, for Chinese migrants to appear consistently in official records. This project embraces those gaps, as even brief traces in the archive reveal that Chinese soldiers were participating in the Civil War. The list, and the information within, will continue to grow as new documents are discovered and cataloged. If you have additional information about any individuals featured in this project, please consider contacting the researcher. History is always a work in progress.
Other Readings

title: Robert Spicer: A Sailor from Shanghai
text: Robert Spicer’s journey to the American Civil War began far from the battlefields of the United States…

title: Robert Spicer: A Sailor from Shanghai
text:
Why his story matters
Spicer’s experience reflects a broader theme explored in this project: how immigrants and minority soldiers used military service to claim recognition in a nation that often viewed them as outsiders.

title: Fighting for Belonging
text: When the American Civil War began in 1861, Chinese immigrants were a small but growing presence in the United States. Many had arrived seeking economic opportunity, especially with the California Gold Rush and the expansion of global trade networks. Few could have imagined that within a few years, they would become a part of the United States’ defining conflict. Yet dozens of Chinese men enlisted in Union and Confederate forces, and their service raises an important question: What did it mean for men who were not fully accepted as American citizens to fight for the nation in military conflict? Beyond a military struggle, the Civil War was a conflict over the meaning of the nation itself–citizenship was not a guarantee, but rather created contested opportunities for negotiation. In fact, marginalized groups of people amidst the greatest heat of war were able to forge a new definition of citizenship. April 16th, 1862 saw the first national draft in American history, which was important for two reasons: 1., introduced a new, special kind of nationalism and allowance of government authority, and 2., foreign men residing in the United States sought consular help about residency and “intentions” to remain. The antebellum U.S. lacked a clear definition of national citizenship, with the states themselves determining most civil and political rights. For years following American colonization, there was an unstated, open door policy for citizenship regulation. There was no system for identification, naturalization, or even passport requirements. Burning questions of loyalty, citizenship, and race determined who could be a member and who was sent to the outskirts.
